section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: To get high purity one needs practically an isotope separation so the simpler conversion unit for 23 production would be very useful.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: Assume 49 is quite useful so that Mr.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: Urey can enrich metal by 25 to 30 or even $40\%$ .

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: About a year ago, a unit was designed tentatively for producing completely enriched 23.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: (Throughout the following discussion "enriched by" signifies that the original concentration of isotope in the metal should be multiplied by the percentage indicated, while "enriched to" indicates the total percent of isotope obtained in the metal.) This design assumed a homogeneous slurry pile using $6\mathrm{kg}$ of metal enriched to $10\%$ ( $600\mathrm{gm}$ ) of 49 or 25.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: The slurry would require 1.2 tons of P-9 of which $75\%$ would be in the pile and $25\%$ in the external pumping system.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: (This design was not calculated independently but was interpolated from larger homogeneous plants to get a unit producing about $10,000\mathrm{kw}$ .)

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: The proposal entails getting 600 gm of 49 per day from W in the form of metal enriched to $10\%$ .

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: The power output of such a series of units per gram of 49 would be very large.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: If pure 49 were used, the concentration of the slurry would be reduced, but it is doubtful whether there would be any practical benefit from the use of pure 49.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: Vernon suggested that, instead of using 49 in the pile and thorium in the reflector, a mixture or solution of thorium and 49 might be used, but Mr.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: Weinberg pointed out that one advantage of 23 was that the purity requirements for 23 are much less stringent than for 49.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: A small conversion pile using surface cooling would produce only 2000 to 3000 kw but this is still a large amount of power from a small amount of metal, although the metal would require frequent replacing.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: Franck asked the total weight of such a unit including the shields and Mr: Weinberg guessed that it would be in the neighborhood of 100 tons.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: Weinberg pointed out that one advantage of a conversion unit would be that it is the equivalent of a pilot plant for a P-9 homogeneous plant.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: Wigner and Weinberg then checked and indicated that since there would be 0.2 gms. per c.c. of metal in the slurry for an unenriched P-9 homogeneous pile, there is a factor of 10 over the concentration in the conversion unit.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: Fermi felt that as long as the concentration was under $10\%$ there was no danger.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: Fermi estimated the gas formation at about 20 liters per second of hydrogen and oxygen.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: Fermi recalled from early experiments that a radium solution held in a flask with a vapor space above it, established its own equilibrium at around 1/2 atmosphere pressure and that the pressure never did go above one atmosphere.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: Burton and Allen who found a $\Gamma = 0.3$ for both fission and $\alpha$ particles.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: Fermi said that about 100 volts were required to dissociate the particles.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: pointed out once more that such a conversion unit would serve as a pilot plant for a P-9 homogeneous pile.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: This would be distinctly a war plant for 49 production.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: Suppose that the Urey metal enriched by about $30\%$ could be produced in large quantities but to carry the concentration further would require a large plant.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: Preliminary calculations for such a reactor indicate a cylindrical pile about 2 meters in diameter by 2 meters high with an enriched core of cylindrical shape, 88 cm in diameter and 1 meter high.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: The core would contain 3 tons of metal enriched by $33\%$ while the surrounding shell would contain 20 tons of ordinary unenriched material.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: The jacket for either the long one piece rods or the series of short sections would be of aluminum having a thickness of about 50 mils.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: The metal in the rods through the core would have a diameter of 1 cm while those in the shell would start at 1 cm diameter near the core and increase gradually to 4 cm in diameter at the periphery.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: This would give a volume ratio of 3 to 1 for the metal in the core.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: The spacing of the rods in the core would be 1.77 cm for a square lattice.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: There would be 1500 rods in the core and somewhat more than that in the shell, depending on the grading.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: The best k for an ordinary water pile is around 0.97 but, with only a slightly optimistic assumption of the ratio of fission to capture cross section (which is uncertain), we can assume a base k of 1.1 for $33\%$ enrichment.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: With a $2\frac{1}{2}\%$ loss from aluminum, k becomes 1.075 and $\Delta = -0.1760 \times 10^{-6}$ .

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: the shell, k would be 0.95.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: As a rough estimate, for each atom of 25 destroyed in the core, we can expect about 0.85 atoms of 49 in the core.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: on the heat transfer which indicate that, assuming a film drop twice that at W or $40^{\circ}$ , and assuming that the water through the pile is all about the same temperature (this seems reasonable since the film drop is the largest item), we can expect around 60,000 kw from such a unit.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: The calculations mentioned above indicate a velocity through the pile of about 10 meters per second.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: This means about 30 tons per second or 430,000 gallons per minute of water through the pile.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: Young's calculation indicates that this make-up might amount to about 0.7 of a ton per second for a $40^{\circ}$ temperature differential.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: The average power in the core per gram of metal is about 10 times the average power for the shell.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: This means the core will produce about 10,000 kw per ton of metal or about five times the utilization at h, although this figure is not far different from the expected utilization considered for P-9 piles.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: If the entire mass of water were pumped through once, it would only be heated $1^{\circ}$ or $2^{\circ}$ which seems wasteful.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: By recirculating, only a small amount of water would be raised about $40^{\circ}$ while the major portion of the water would remain at the same temperature.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: Cooper said that there was no question in a P-9 scheme that boiling was not the most efficient way of removing the heat but that in a pile requiring such large volumes of water for removing the heat, boiling might be more attractive.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: Young's calculations had indicated that this method would only get out about 1/3 of the power and that there was no great gain in adding a circulation system to a boiling scheme.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: Wigner felt that for enrichments higher than $30\%$ , Mr.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: Weinberg's water pile was not so good but that for enrichments up to $30\%$ it probably was better than Mr.

section: Notes On Meeting Of Friday, May 12, 1944
text: Fermi felt there was a reasonably good chance that $30\%$ enriched material may become available and agreed with Mr.
